text: Assalam-o-Alaikum everyone. I am Dr. Tayyab.

Today, I will be showing you a video on how to perform laparoscopic appendectomy with only two ports with the assistance of this various needle. Technically, I have used two ports. One is the optical and other is the working port.

but clinically after two weeks when the wounds are healed there is no scar because the only scar of one working port that is in hypogastric region it is not visible and i have used the scandavian technique for optical procar so that scar is also not visible So, if we say that it is scarless aconductomy, that won't be wrong.
How I have used this varus needle for varus assisted to quote aconductomy, there is a video link in the description.

You can also find the other uses, its every detail about this varus needle in that video. Before going to the surgery video, kindly like and subscribe my YouTube channel for more upcoming surgery videos.
and the details about laparoscopic tools. Thank you.
The surgeon inserts the optical trocar using the Scandinavian technique through an incision in the infraumbilical crease.
There is another video of mine describing the detailed Scandinavian technique and the link is given in the description along with its benefits.
And I personally like the Scandinavian technique for primary trocar placement.
The surgeon uses artery forceps to dilate the tract of the obliterated vitrointestinal duct, visibly opening the loose areolar tissue.
So you can insert the varus needle directly before dilating the tract or you can insert it after its dilatation. It's your own choice.

The surgeon uses a modified Scandinavian technique to directly insert the optical trocar, demonstrating the method of holding the ports. After insertion, the flow rate is adjusted. The primary trocar is then inserted in the suprapubic region under direct vision, serving as the only working port.
Your index finger should always be parallel with the long axis of the port and should be pointing towards the skin to prevent any inadvertent injury with the momentum of the port.
The surgeon inserts a laparoscopic grasper through the suprapubic port, using it as the sole instrument for tissue manipulation and movements during the appendectomy procedure.

The surgeon locates the appendix by following the tinea coli from the cecum. The appendix appears long and acutely inflamed. The surgeon prepares to insert the varus needle for dissection and appendectomy using a single working port.

The surgeon makes a 2mm incision for the insertion of the varus needle, carefully choosing the insertion point to allow maximum manipulation while ensuring gentle insertion to avoid injury from the needle's sharp bevel.
After that I have removed the steelet. There is a detailed video of varus assisted appendectomy, how to do it, what instruments are required and the link is given in the description.
The surgeon passes a loop through the varus needle using a custom-made loop passer, utilizing purlin thread for the procedure.
The surgeon removes the loop passer and inserts a loop for easier handling. Using a grasper, the surgeon passes through the loop to grasp the acutely inflamed appendix.

The surgeon carefully monitors the tip of the varus needle to avoid puncture injuries to the bowel or other intraperitoneal structures. The appendix is fed through the loop, and the surgeon prepares to identify the point for dissection.
The surgeon loosens the loop around the appendix and repositions it for better handling, rotating the appendix clockwise and securing it again with the needle.

The surgeon uses a Ligasure device to perform dissection around the appendix, noting the ease and time efficiency of this tool. Due to the length of the appendix, the surgeon loosens the loop and repositions it at a more proximal point for better manipulation and handling.

The surgeon holds the appendix at a more proximal point and performs further dissection towards its base. The appendix is visibly attached to the lateral abdominal wall, requiring careful dissection in that area.
The surgeon has reached the base of the appendix, using the varus needle to cook and cut the remaining mesoappendix. The appendix is now completely freed from its mesoappendix.

The surgeon brings the thread end of Vicryl 1-0 into the surgical field for extracorporeal knot tying. The thread is wound around the base of the appendix, and the knot is tightened using a knot pusher. The base of the appendix is ligated, and the thread is cut, completing this step of the appendectomy with the assistance of the varus needle.

The surgeon cuts the appendix at a safe distance from the ligation point. Using a grasper, the appendix is fed into a 10 mm port for removal via the optical trocar, employing a rail-loading technique.

The surgeon uses artery forceps to grasp and pull out the appendix through the optical trocar. The optical trocar wound is then irrigated with normal saline and pyridine. A post-operative view shows the healed trocar wound below the waistline, indicating minimal scarring.
